Introduction
Welcome to module 6. This is the last full out module and this one is absolutely
packed with great stuff. First, we’re going to cover your sales video. Again, you don’t
have to do a sales video. You could do a sales letter. But most people are going to find
that sales video is actually easier to do, it’s easier to be authentic in your message and
just generally works a little better. In any case I’m going to walk you through a couple
of real-life examples of sales videos from very successful launches and you’re going to
see that you don’t have to be some amazing sales person to do this.
Then, after we go through the whole sales video, I’m going to walk you through
the joint venture launches. The deal to join venture launches is that, the analogy I like
it’s like pouring gasoline on a fire, because in the joint venture launch also you can tap
into the power of other people’s lists. You’ll see how you get joint venture partners, and
how you can do one of those launches to get massive leverage with your JB launch.
Now, again, of course you don’t have to do a JV launch. I’ve got lots of PLF
owners who’ve done great without ever doing one. But it’s something you’re going to
want to learn about, because it can be very, very powerful.
We have a lot of ground to cover in this module, but I want you to remember,
and you’ve heard me say this before, it’s all about the baby steps. You just need to take
it one video at a time, go through one video at a time and just keep on making that
incremental progress. Because the incremental progress just adds up to exponential
gain and exponential progress in the long run. You’ve got to start where you’re at and
you take one step at a time.
Of course if you’re still with me at this point then you’ve heard that before, and if
you’re still with me at this point then you win. I love all my PLF owners. But the fact
that you made it to module six here, I have to tell you that the vast majority of people
will never get that far. They’ll never stick with it, they’ll never make it this far into the
program, they’ll never see this video. You are one of a very select, select number. Just
stop and think about how awesome you are. Congratulations on making it this far. It
really is a big deal. Now it’s time to push forward, it’s time to finish this module up and
put your launch together.
Remember, this product launch formula is a proven formula. It’s been proven
thousands of times. You’ve already put in most of the work here. Now you just need to
keep on taking those baby steps and put your launch together.
What I want you to do now is just scroll down and leave a comment for me,
because it is a big deal that you’ve made it here, that you’re here in module six with
me. Tell me that you’ve made it all the way to module six. I really want to hear it from

How to Sell
! Now I want to talk about your sales video or it could be a sales letter but we’re
mostly going to talk about sales video. There’s one absolute thing that you must do and
this is the most important thing. This is probably the biggest mistake I see in sales
letters and sales videos so I’m going to walk you through the whole thing. We’re going
to give you step by step on how to do this. The most important thing if you get this
right, then it’s game over you win, is you need to focus on the transformation. What I
mean by that is your prospect when they step up and purchase from you then they’re
life is going to change in some way. That’s why you’re selling them something. There’s
going to be some transformation in their life. If you don’t like that word think about a
change. How is your life going to change?
What is the impact that’s going to be on their life once they purchase your
product? It’s all about the transformation. It’s not about the stuff, it’s not about
whatever they get, the goop or the DVD’s or the CD’s or the widget or whatever, it’s
about how their life’s actually going to change. That’s really what they want. If you look
at Product Launch Formula, when I sold this product whenever I sell Product Launch
Formula I don’t talk about you’re going to get a bunch of videos, you’re going to get a
bunch of modules, you’re going to get this, you’re going to get that. I talk about how
it’s going to change their life. They’re going to be able to launch a business. They’re
going to be able to launch a product and have that instant momentum. They’re not
going to really be dependent on hope marketing. That’s what I mean about the
transformation.
If you’re teaching people how to play signs on a guitar, it’s not that you’re going
to send them a book and a DVD, you’re going to give them the ability to be able to play
songs for themselves and for their friends. They’re ability to entertain people, to be the
center of a party. The self-worth that they’re going to have from being able to play
songs. If you’re going to teach them how to train their dog, again it’s not some DVD’s,
it’s not some videos, it’s not a forum, it’s having this dog that will come instantly when
you call them every time. That greater joy that they’ll have in their life because of their
better relationship with their dog. If you’re going to teach them how to play tennis it’s
again, not about what the actual stuff is, it’s the ability to walk on the court and have
the confidence and win that match.

-- 3 of 55 --

The stuff doesn’t matter or it might matter a little bit but it only matters 10%.
You got to focus on the transformation. If you forget everything else but you get this
right then you’re going to end up with a great sales message, a great sales video.
Another thing is don’t assume that everyone who’s going to see your sales video has
seen your prelaunch content. That they’ve been actually through your launch. In an
ideal world they will have watched all your pieces of prelaunch content, they’ll have
watched them twice. They’ll have memorized them but the reality is people are busy
and they don’t always watch every single one of your prelaunch videos. Or they might
not remember them or they’ll skip through them or they’ll see a beginning and they’ll
turn it off.
You can’t assume, don’t go into the sales letter or the sales video and just start
making the sale. You have to open with the big promise. If you go back to your first
piece of prelaunch content, hopefully you open up that with a big promise and you grab
their attention. You have to do the same thing with your sales video. You don’t just
come into the sales video and say well you saw my prelaunch content, this is great stuff
and this is what it costs. You have to grab their attention and you have to revisit that
overall promise and the overall launch theme. That doesn’t mean you’re going to
completely repeat your prelaunch content. This isn’t like you did three 18 minute videos
and now you’re going to just repeat them and then ask for their money. This is a sales
video, I’m going to walk you through the process, but you can’t assume they’ve seen
that promise, they’ve seen that theme that you had in your prelaunch.
You don’t want to get overly complicated. It’s really easy to get way too complex
with these things and you don’t want to be too boring. In fact, you don’t want to be
boring at all. In terms of length a lot of times people worry about the length, how long
should it be and this might sound a little simplistic but you need to make it as long as it
needs to be and not one second longer. You need to tell your whole story, the whole
story of your product. You need to tell that promise, the story of your product, why it’s
going to change their lives, how it’s going to transform their lives and so you have to be
very complete there but you’ve got to make it as short as you can within that context.
I’ve had sales videos myself that were 30, 40 maybe even close to 50 minutes long and
I still sweat every single second and every single minute. I’m trying to make it as short
as possible even if it’s 47 minutes long.
In that case it was a product that needed that length of sales video. On the other
hand, I’m going to show you some examples here of sales videos that were more like in
that 10 minute, 12 minute range and they were very effective. There’s an old saying in
copy writing that your sales letter, and we’re going to say sales video in this case, your
sales video can never be too long it can only be too boring. You got to make it as long
as it needs to be and not any longer, don’t be boring. If you’re teaching about how to
play golf and your best friend is not a golfer, don’t run the sales video by them for their
feedback. Don’t run it by your spouse if your spouse is not a golfer, it has to be
interesting to the people that you’re selling to. I’ll guarantee you, a really good sales
video for a product about golf to a non-golfer is going to put them to sleep immediately
and they’ll tell you it’s absolutely too long but they’re not interested in the market.

You have to keep your avatar in mind. Finally, as a bit of a warm up here, you do
not want to sell from you heels. By that I mean leaning back, deer in the headlights,
you’re scared about it. You don’t want to be selling from there; you want to be selling
from power. First of all you’ve got to have a great product. If you don’t have a great
product you don’t need to bother. Make sure it’s a great product and if you do it like the
seed launch taught you, you’re going to have a great product. If you were very
interactive in your product and always tap into the market and what the market needs
then you have a great product. We’re going to assume you’ve got that great product
what you need to do before you shoot the sales video, this is really important.
Before you shoot the sales video, I want you to meditate. (I use that term
because that’s something I do in my life, I meditate.) But if you don’t like that word just
think about how your product is going to change your avatars live. Think about the
impact you are going to have on them. Really get real, think about a real person, your
real avatar. How’s it going to impact their life? How’s it going to impact your life now,
immediately when they get your product? How’s it going to impact their life in three
months? In one year? In five years? How’s it going to change their life? Think about
who they’re going to become. The change is throughout the entire length. Even if you
teach him how to play tennis, if all of a sudden they became a more confident tennis
player they become a more confident person.
Think about the big impact it’s going to have on them. Who are they going to
become? Think about how their life will change. Think about the positive impact they’re
going to have on other people. If all of a sudden they become a better tennis player
and a more confident person maybe they become a better parent, a better friend, a
better spouse. Think about all the ripples throughout their life and the ripples that
they’ll have on other people. Then think about what will happen if they don’t get your
product. If they don’t become that tennis player, if they don’t become a more confident
person; think about what it will cost them. Then think about what if they get someone
else’s lousy product that’s nowhere near as good as yours? How will that mess up their
life? How will that set them back?
You really want to think about those things and really get super clear on that and
spend five minutes, ten minutes really … I’m going to say it again, meditating on that;
really focusing on those things before you shoot your video. Then you’re going to be
able to sell from a place of power. That is the place you want to be selling from, not
from your heels, you want to be selling from certainty and from power. You do that, you
get there by focusing on that transformation that your product is going to have. Make it
real in your mind and then you’re going to be able to step in front of the camera and
just knock it out of the park.

Sales Video Mechanics
! Let’s talk about our sales video here. I’m going to give you a couple of absolutely
key rules here that you need to follow. If you only this, do anything else right, then
you’re going to be just fine.
!
Rule number 1 is that your offer in your sales video, it’s all about the
transformation, it’s not about the stuff. It’s about how your prospect’s life is going to be
transformed. It’s like what impact will your product make on them?
!
What change will they have in their life? I don’t care if you’re selling golf clubs or
you’re selling a product about how to play guitar or you’re selling a product about how
to sell real estate, or how to have a massage therapy practice, it doesn’t matter.
!
It’s still about the transformation. Your product is about the transformation. It’s
how your prospect, once they buy your product, how is your life going to change? How
is it going to be better? How are you doing to increase their pleasure or decrease their
pain? It’s not about whether you deliver it on a bunch of DVDs or it’s in a book or it’s in
a seminar or a webinar or it’s a widget or whatever, it’s not so much about the stuff, it’s
about the transformation.
!
The stuff is far, far less important. The actual deliverables, how they get it in
their hands isn’t nearly as important as what the transformation is. Think about like if
you had the cure for cancer, you were selling the cure for cancer, would it matter if it
was a pill that they took or if it’s a shot that they took or if they had to sit in a machine
for a half an hour or whatever. It doesn’t matter how it’s delivered.
!
In the example of like cancer and like if it’s radiation treatment and if they have
to go through is a horrible treatment for weeks on end, well, then the stuff does matter.
For the most part, it’s the end result that’s really important. You have to focus on that in
your sales video.
!
Rule number 2 is to refer back to rule number 1. In other words it’s all about
communicating the transformation. That’s what your sales video exists to do, that’s the
reason for it, is to communicate their transformation and then ask for the money.
!
A lot of people really concerned about how long their sales video should be. I
don’t want to be flippant but this is important. Don’t think, “Wow! Jeff’s got a 27 minute
video so I have to make my video 27 minutes long.” or “So and so had a 90 minute
video and I have to make my video 90 minutes long.” or “So and so had a 50 minute
video so I need to make my video 50 minutes long.”
!

-- 6 of 55 --

The deal is your video should be as long as it needs to be and to tell the
complete story of your product and to make your offer and then not one second longer.
Really, this goes for all your videos whether they’re pre-launched videos or sales videos.
My pre-launch videos hadn’t been anywhere from, I don’t know, 15 minutes up
to 55 minutes long and it’s just because of what I was trying to accomplish, the story I
was trying to tell in those videos took different amounts of time. I’ll tell you what, when
I had the video that was 55 minutes long, I was doing everything I could to cut that as
much as I could.
!
I’ve put in all kinds of effort just to take 30 seconds out of it. It’s like 54 minutes
and 30 seconds instead of 55 minutes. I’m always trying to make as concise and
compact as possible but I’m not going to sacrifice the story. I’m not going to sacrifice
making a good video. There’s this old saying in copywriting that your copy can never be
too long, it can only be too boring. As long as you keep your video tight and engaging
and you make it as compact as you can to tell your story and make your offer that’s
fine.
!
That being said, I’m going to give you some guidelines here to maybe shoot for.
If you’re just starting out, I would aim for the 10 to 20 mark and then once I get some
experience, if you get some experience, you can feel free to go up to 35 minutes. Most
of my sales videos these days have been in that 27 to 30 minute range. For a high end,
product, like product launch formula, that’s what it takes me to get it done. You can go
up to there.
!
That being said, recently I came out with my seed launch, deep diver course
which was basically an additional course just on seed launches, really deep on seed
launches. Now, I think I got that sales video done somewhere between 7 and 8
minutes. I’m not always going to make a 27 video just because I can.
!
Continuing on, once you really become expert, maybe go up to 40 minutes and if
you’d become just an amazing uber pro you could go up to 50 minutes. The reason why
more experience can go longer is you have more experience at staying engaging and
not being boring. The thing is is just because you’re an uber pro doesn’t mean you
make a 50 minute video.
!
Like I said, I would consider myself either an expert or an uber pro and mine
come in usually for big products like product launch formula, 27 to 30 minutes long. It
seems to be what I’ve been doing. Like I said, for the seed launch deep dive course, we
got it done somewhere between 7 and 8 minutes. I’m going to make it as tight as
possible but there’s just some rough guidelines for you.
!
Here’s how to script out your video. We’re going to break this down in bullet, you
don’t always have to file this perfectly but this is a good starting place. First of all, you
open up with the big promise. You want to paint the transformation. You then to really

-- 7 of 55 --

be able to feel that big promise, that opportunity for their life to change. Then you want
to show authority, especially if you can show results. Often, in a launch you can
basically use that prelaunch content and talk about their pre-launch content and how
many views it had, how many comments it had, maybe you can flash up little screen
shots of the prelaunch videos. Because just publishing great pre-launch content shows
great authority.
!
Basically, you want to go opportunity than authority. This is a great place for
proof and testimonials. Start to pull in story whether it’s your story or the story of your
students. This is like generally a pretty good chunk of the video in here. You want to
foreshadow urgency if you can. Basically, everyone would love to put off any buying
decision. People just naturally progress in buying decisions. You want to have scarcity or
urgency in your offer.
!
Right here, just a foreshadow, you can just mention it that this is only a limited
time and we’re going to get going, we’re going to have to start our class soon or
something like that. I’ll often find some way to foreshadow the urgency early on. Then,
you’ve basically, here’s the deal, you make your offer and you cover your features and
your benefits in here.
!
Here, often, you do talk about your stuff to some degree. Even when you’re
talking about your stuff, you want to keep on focusing on the transformation. If they
get 5 modules and in module 1 is this, this and this, well, what do those things actually
do for the people? They don’t care that there’s 4 videos, but here we’re going to do the
4 videos that show you how to play the F chord on the guitar.
!
Actually, this is the chord that so many people struggle with, but when you learn
an F chord, it unlocks all these different songs, yadi-yadi-yada. Even when you’re talking
about what they actually get, the stuff, you always want to focus on the transformation.
!
In here, you can offer bonuses and then do the revelation of price or you can flip
those around. You could do revelation appraise first and then the bonuses. Generally, I
like to do the bonuses, talk about the bonuses that they get. Then you reveal the price.
When you reveal the price, it’s not like … price justification there and it’s not like you’re
saying, “Oh man! It’s $299.” “I know that sounds like a lot of money but it’s really not
because I give you this, this and this.”
!
That’s not the way you do it, you want to talk about price from a position of
authority and the price justification means, you often want to do apples to oranges
comparison here where you’re comparing your … let’s say, if it’s a knowledge base
course and information course, you can compare it to what a college course cost. You
could compare it to what it would cost to hire you on a 1 on 1 basis a coach. Those are
really good comparisons. What it would cost in real world experience, if you went out
and worked for 10 years to get this experience, what would that cost you?

!
What it would cost you if you went out and tried to do your job or do this thing
or get this accomplishment, this transformation without any coaching, what would it
cost?
If you looked at product launch formula, I think it gives you a far better
marketing education than any college degree, any marketing degree from any
university. I say that if someone who went to a pretty nice university and got a business
degree, I think you got a better business education with product launch formula.
!
You know what? If you go to a business school it’s going to cost you tens of
thousands of dollars. That all of a sudden makes product launch formula look pretty
darn inexpensive, right? You want to do some appraise revelation and a justification
appraise.
!
Then here’s what to do now which you’re going to basically describe to them the
process they’re going to go through to actually buy from you. I’m going to want to,
“Hey, you’re going to click on that button after you get to that button, you get to a
form. Put in your details, after you put in your details then you hit the enter button and
we’ll hook you up right away and we’ll ship the product out overnight or you’ll get
instant access to the membership sight, something like that.
!
Then you have to create urgency. This is scarcity, why should they act now?
You’ve always need some type of urgency. Even if that’s, only that, “Hey, we have this
launch special here and we’re only going to keep this launch special open a few days
and after that then either the price is going to go up or these bonuses are going to go
away or I’m going to pull the offer off the market and you’re not going to be able to get
anymore.
!
After urgency is your call to action. This is where you basically tell them now is
the time to buy. You get all these things, your life is going to change and this way if you
get these bonuses, it’s an amazing deal. You have to act now so click the button and
we’ll get you all hooked up. Then you can come out with a surprise bonus, an extra
bonus and then that gives you … after the extra bonus, and you don’t always have to
do this, you don’t always have to have that extra bonus but it’s nice because then it
sets you up to reclose.
!
Basically, you come back, you have that call to action where you’re closing on
them, where you say, “Now is the time to act. Click that button. Oh! And yeah, before
you go, I got one more thing. I got this extra, super amazing extra super bonus so go
ahead and click that button now and I’ll see you on the other side. That is the basic
sales video script. I’m going to be going through some examples for you so you can see
how a lot of this has done. But that’s the overall script for your sales video.
!

I’ve got a few notes for you to follow here. One thing is do not assume that your
viewer watched your pre-launched content. Your sales video needs to be complete. In
other words, if in your pre-launch video you basically explained why this is so important
and you explain about that their transformation, how their life is going to change and
you just can’t start off a sale just saying, “Okay now, I’m going to give you this product
with this widget and this stuff and this it costs this amount of money. You can’t do that.
You have to tell the complete story of your offer and your product and the
transformation before you go through and you make your offer and you reveal the price
and all that stuff. Don’t assume that they watched your pre-launch content.
!
A lot of them, they might have gotten the link, they might even clicked that link
but they didn’t watch it all or they just never saw it, those e-mails got lost or they were
offline and out of touch or whatever. Your sales video needs to be complete.
!
You need to paint the transformation. By that, by paint the transformation, I
mean make it real. Think of it like there’s a blank canvas and you’re actually making a
painting what to tell their life is going to be transformed. How are you going to take a
pain away from them? How are you going to give them more pleasure? How are you
going to make their life easier, more convenient, more fun? Paint the transformation.
!
Before you shoot the video, I really want you to think about and even meditate
on how your product will transform their life. The value that will get in their life
immediately in 3 months from now, 12 months from now, 5 years from now. How it’s
going to make them a better, happier, more fulfilled person? How is going to make the
people around them that they know even happier or make their lives better?
!
What it would cost them to not have your product? What it would cost them if
they bought someone else’s product and their product was lousy and it ruined their life?
I need you really just focus at how you’re going to transform their life. The reason I
want you to do this is because I want you to shoot that video with all kinds of
confidence and authority, I don’t want you selling from your heels. I want you selling
from power.
!
A lot of people, they think they have a hang up about asking for the money.
Believe me, I was there as well. This is something I struggle with. When it came to
asking for the money I was a little squeamish years ago. In doing the work, I just focus
on the amazing results that people get. I just look at product launch formula and the
thousands of lives that I have changed through my teachings.
!
I think of people like Susan Garret, like John Gallagher who have grown their
businesses enormously because they got my product. They have an incredibly different,
much better life now that they wouldn’t have had before. I want you to be thinking
about the same thing, about that transformation you’re going to make.
!

-- 10 of 55 --

Then when you think about that, that’s how you get passionate and that’s how
you get powerful in your sales video. You need to speak with confidence, don’t be
apologetic. Come out and tell them how their life is going to change and why they need
to get your product.
!
Look into the camera lens but don’t stare at it. When you’re talking to someone
in a conversation, they are not locked on you staring at you unwaveringly all the time.
They’re looking at you but they’re blinking their eyes. Sometimes they might like break
your eye contact and look away for a little bit. Feel free to do that, you should do that.
You should be as natural as possible on camera.
!
Don’t talk too fast. Sometimes people have this tendency to … once that camera
goes on they just … your blood pressure goes up, your pulse goes up and you start
talking really fast. Don’t talk too fast, you want to be understandable. On the other
side, don’t slow down intentionally. Don’t go super slow. Some people think that you
sound like you’ve got more authority if you talk really slow. No, it just sounds like you’re
trying to talk slow.
!
You just want to talk at your normal pace. Don’t go too fast, don’t go too slow
and have high energy. You need to have … and some people are just naturally lower
energy, more relaxed. I’m a pretty relaxed person. But when you’re doing that video,
you think about their transformation, get pumped up, get passionate, move around,
change your state. Get into a heightened state. Even if you job in place, jump and
down for … I do that before I shoot ever video, I jump up and down in place for 10 or

Open Cart Considerations
!
I want to talk about your actual sequence of your open cart. This is more about
e-mails than how you’re messaging your list.
You want to leave your cart open anywhere from three to seven days. I think for
most people five to seven days is that golden period. If you go longer than seven days,
in my opinion, it ends up turning into a beg-a-thon because you're going to want to
keep up the momentum and keep up the pressure in continuing to e-mail throughout
your open cart. Even at seven days usually at the end of it it feels like you're running
on fumes, you been sending the same type of e-mails over and over so that's why I like
to do it five to seven days. You could do shorter and I think it's more of an advance
technique to go shorter. A lot of times I have gone a day and a half, two days, maybe
three days for my launches but I've done a lot of them.
The thing is with the shorter launches it gives you less time to adjust if things
are not going the way you want them to go. That sales video just really isn't all that
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page
!
30
PLF_Module6v2.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 30 of 55 --

good, it's not converting as well as I want so I'm going to re-shoot the sales video.
Well if you've only got the cart open for two or three days it's hard to do something like
that. If you have it open five to seven days you can adjust. Some partners that didn't
get their e-mail out for some reason, that's fine, they can adjust. So shorter period
launches they really build scarcity and position in moving forward in the market but
things can go wrong. I think five to seven days is that golden period.
Then favor opening days are Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday. Friday also works.
I've actually had a lot of Friday opens and they've worked just fine. You generally don't
want to open on the weekends. Now this is market dependent; there are some market
where it's better to open on the weekends. I've heard the dating advice market works
really great on Saturday because all these guys are home on Saturdays wishing they
had a date. So different markets can react differently but across most markets,
Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, specifically Tuesday and Thursday are good days to
open up.
Favorite closing days in general for most markets you want to avoid the
weekends. If you are going to close on the weekend then don't do it on Saturday, close
on Sunday. Also you want to close at midnight. You don't want to close in the middle
of the day. Close at midnight otherwise you're leaving money on the table.
Here are some of the ways I have structured it and there's a lot of different
ways, lot of flexibility. Open Monday close Friday, that's a five day launch. I've done
that recently, it's worked out pretty well. Open Tuesday close Thursday, that's a short
one, a three day launch. I've opened Wednesday and closed Friday. That's a three day
launch as well. It's just moved one day there so you see how flexible you can be. I've
opened up on Tuesday and closed on the following Monday. That's a seven day launch
that bridged across a weekend. I've opened on Thursday and closed on Monday, that's
a five day launch that bridged a weekend. I've opened on Thursday and closed on
Wednesday, a seven day launch that bridged the weekend.
Obviously you can move these around. All these are five or seven or three. Can
you do six or four? Absolutely. You can open on Thursday and close on Tuesday, that's
a six day launch. The reality is most people are on mine almost every day. In general
e-mails get opened more on Tuesday and Wednesdays and Thursdays than they do on
Mondays or Saturdays. In general, in most markets. But you can test this out. If
you're just watching your e-mails and watching when the open rates tend to be, you'll
see what are the good days in your market. But this isn't rocket science and it isn't
science whatsoever, there is a lot of flexibility. Don't worry about which days, don't
worry about that, it's not important, just get the open right and you'll be fine.
Now, when do you send e-mails during a launch, or during your open cart? And
the answer is you generally send them a lot more than people think you send them. So
on your cart opening day, you want to send two e-mails. Actually I usually send three
e-mails. I send an e-mail before the cart opens giving people the final instructions,
telling them what to expect once the cart opens, and then you send the old, "We're
open, go buy it, click here, go check it out, you been looking forward to it." So that's a

-- 31 of 55 --

very short e-mail - 15 words, "Hey we're open, click here." It can be very, very short e-
mail. Hopefully they're anticipating that.
Then maybe three, four, five hours after you open cart you want to send the
tsunami e-mail. This is something I've been teaching since the first version of PLF
probably around 2005. The tsunami e-mail is this e-mail where you're giving the
impression, and this is often the case, that you're just trying to keep your head above
water. That this whole thing has become this epic event that's carrying, threatening to
carry you away. The feeling in this e-mail is we're all in this together, hang in there, I'm
just trying to stay one step ahead of you and keep the server up, and make sure
everything runs well.
On opening cart day there's often some quirky thing happens that you don't
expect that throws a curve ball at you. Use that as a reason to e-mail people and say,
"Hey, you might have had some trouble getting through the site," or "We had this link
that wasn't working, thanks for hanging in there. The good news is we've got
everything fixed, and orders going through OK, lots of people have already jumped on
board." If it's a digital product, "Some people are already in the membership site and
they're building a great community." That's the second e-mail on opening day.
Then you want to e-mail every single day during your launch. So if you have a
seven day open cart, that means you send those two e-mails on day one. Then day
two, three, four, five, and six you're sending an additional e-mail every single day.
Often I'm sending an FAQ e-mail, I might send a little bonus video, but you just keep on
coming up with reasons to mail them. That's why I say, once you get that launch
longer than seven days, it's hard to keep on coming up with reasons to keep on mailing
them. You can send e-mails talking about some of the objections and answering some
of the objections. Talk about what's happening with the people that actually bought
your product, You've actually got it in their hands now, the results you're getting, or the
feedback you're getting from them.
Then the day before you close cart, you want to start talking about the scarcity.
When I say close cart, not everyone is going to close their shopping cart and take their
product off the market. Lot of people do with their launches but there always has to be
some definitive end to your launch. You had an open and then you have a close for the
launch. The product might stay on the market but something bad happens if they don't
buy. You create a deadline at the end of your launch.
Generally there's three ways to create scarcity. First is that bonuses go away so
the offer gets less attractive. You put together this bonus package for the launch and
the bonuses go away. The second is the price goes up. There's this [inaudible 08:30]
launch special, you can get it for $97, after the launch it's going to be $197. So that's
reason number two or scarcity reason number two. The third one is the project comes
off the market, you're actually going to close the cart and they aren't going to be able
to get it. Those three, I named them in that order, that's the order of power. From the
least powerful to the most powerful. The least powerful is the bonuses go away. Still

powerful, still good, scarcity, but that's less powerful than the price is going up. Price is
going up is strong but it's not as strong as the projects actually coming off the market.
Actually you can combine all three. You can say hey, "We're going to close the
cart and after we close the cart, we might in the future, bring this back on the market.
When we do, if we do, all these bonuses, Bonus A, B, and C that I told you about, we're
not going to offer those ever again. And when we bring it back the price is going to go
up to X. You got to act by tonight or else it's coming off the market and if we ever
release it again, you're not going to be able to get these bonuses, and the price will go
up to X. That's combining all three.
But any way, the day before you close cart you start messaging scarcity. If
you're going to close on a Friday, you open on Monday you send two e-mails on
Monday, you send another e-mail on Tuesday, one on Wednesday, one on Thursday.
You might even send two on Thursday. Send one early in the morning, that might be
an FAQ, or a little bit of content, or answering objection. But then on midday on
Thursday, you would send something that says hey, "We're going to be closing down
tomorrow. If you want to jump on board you got to act now, we're closing tomorrow at
midnight." And you do want to close at midnight. I think I mentioned that already.
You don't want to close in the middle of the day. So you close at midnight.
So the day before cart close, you want to start messaging scarcity, possibly send
two e-mails on that day. Then on closing day you want to send two or three e-mails.
Lot of people are like, "Jeff I can't send two e-mails. I've never sent two e-mails on the
same day before." The thing is you don't have to send two e-mails if you want. The
only thing is you're going to leave half your sales on the table. Because half of your
sales likely will come in on your last day. That's why it's important to mail multiple
times.
Three e-mails, how do you actually send three e-mails to your list? Well at six
am. You send an e-mail says you're closing tonight at midnight. Midnight whatever
Pacific time, Eastern time, whatever you want, we're closing tonight at midnight. Then
in the middle of the day you send another e-mail that says we're going to close in 12
hours. Then at six pm. You send them, "This is your last chance." Often I'll send a
thank you.
This last one will be, "Hey I just wanted to . . . Wow this has been a crazy
experience, I've put out all these videos, got all these comments, it's just been a
wonderful experience to go through, thanks for sharing it with me. Thank you for
everyone whose already jumped on board with me. We've already got tons of people in
the membership site and they're all creating this great community together. Oh by the
way, if you haven't jumped on board you still have a few hours to go and we're closing
tonight at midnight." That's how you do three e-mails on the final day.
You definitely follow this. At times people pull a punch. You got to go for the
jugular, you have to close this thing down with power and this is exactly how you do it.
Send two e-mails on that first day, send an additional e-mail every day your open, the

JV Launch
! Okay. Now we're going to talk about joint venture launches. Joint venture
launches, boy, they can get pretty complex, but at the most basic, what it is is a joint
venture partner of yours or an affiliate, they will mail their list to go and look at your
pre-launch content, then the people from their list will go through your pre-launch, buy
your product, and then you'll give that joint venture partner some portion of the sales,
the proceeds, the commission.
The great thing about this is it's just a way to all of a sudden put yourself in front
of a lot of people. Just think about it. You might have a list of 1000 people that you've
built up over months or a year or whatever, but if you can find a partner that's got
10,000 people on their list, it's like, all of a sudden, you have extended your influence in
a big way. Joint venture launches can be really lucrative. I've personally made millions,
in fact, I've had tens of millions of dollars in sales in affiliate and JV launches. Really
powerful stuff.
Now, it sounds awesome but there's a lot of "gotchas," and it's not like a quick
fix in general. What I'm going to give you is here in Parkland's Formula, as you've seen
throughout all of Parkland's Formula, it's all about the long term, having a long-term
win. It's not a get rich quick, and you can't look at joint venture partners as a get rich
quick type of thing. This is very much a holistic approach to your business. It's a longer-
term approach, but it absolutely works.
The first thing you've got to do is, you've got to build out your internal launch
first, and you need to get your metrics and make sure it works. Let's draw this out.
We're going to say that this is your launch. Okay. You've got three pieces of pre-launch
content. Goes like that. Here's Number One, Number Two, Number Three, and your
sales video. Typical classic internal launch.
What you want to do, if you remember back to that Circle of Awesome, which I
covered earlier in PLF, is this idea where you do your internal launch first. You figure out
what your metrics are, and you make sure the launch works, and then you move
forward and you do a joint venture launch, because what a joint venture launch is, is if
this is your internal launch, then say you have a joint venture partner out here with a

list. What they would do is they would mail into a squeeze page, and the people that

If this person has a 10,000 person list and maybe they sent 1000 visitors to your
squeeze page, and you've got a really great squeeze page, just to make the math
easier, at a 50% opt-in rate, all of a sudden you would have 500 people going through
your launch. That is an awesome thing.
Now, think if you had five of those joint venture partners, and they each had
those 10,000 person lists, and they're all mailing into your squeeze page. You can see

What you're going to do is you're going to track via software, and we'll get into that a

This is the best list building. I can't remember if I said this, but I've done this
over and over, where I've added tens of thousands of people to my list within a couple
of weeks. It's very powerful. That's how I've built my list. It's the absolute best way to
do it.
Now, like I said, there is some complexities here, and it doesn't work for every

your relationship with them, and they're not going to come back and promote for you in

Everything I teach is about building a long-term business that works going

This is not a short-term game. I have partners that have been promoting ever

Of course, to do any of this, you have to get these partners on board, and so

so they're willing to mail for you. If someone's got a 10,000 person list, they'e got a lot

How do you find partners? This might sound really simplistic, but Google is one
of the greatest ways. Frankly, if you're in a market, you've been in that market for a
while, you probably have a good idea who your competitors are, and I use that term
"competitors." I really think of them not as competitors but as future partners. Who in
your market are the big dogs? Who are the people that are publishing? Who are the

Create a list of those. I use a spreadsheet to do this. Then, go to Google and do

building a list, they're probably not going to be a great venture partner or a great

Go ahead and join their list. You probably want to put together, get a separate

program. When you go to their Website, just do a search on their page and see if
they've got an affiliate program. Join their affiliate program. Watch what they send out
to their affiliates. Watch what they send out to their main list.
Then, like I said, create that spreadsheet and track them. Track who promotes
who, who's sending good Emails, who's not doing a very good job of following up. You
really want to understand what the JV landscape is like within your market. Are these
top five all promoting each other? Or are they mostly, people aren't promoting each
other? You really need to focus in on that overall JV landscape.
Other ways to find them is, once you start to get that list of potential partners in
Google, another way is to go to live events. I'm a big fan of going to live events to meet
potential partners. You need to basically go there, and you're looking for who the
players are. Who are the people that have lists and are getting results? Go to ClickBank
and search on their marketplace. This doesn't work for all products and all markets, but
a lot of them, you can take a look at the marketplace and ClickBank and see what
products they're selling.
They have this measure called "Gravity," and that basically tells you what
products are selling well. Go in there and see who's got really good products that are
selling really well.
These are some of the ways that you can start to figure out, who are the
players? Who can really make an impact in your market? One of the things is, like I
said, I want you to go through the top 50 sites and find out who's building a list and
start building relationships, but the reality is is you only need one partner.

JV Launch, Part 2
How do you build relationships with joint venture partners or potential joint venture
partners?
I mentioned this in the last video - it’s all about adding value.
I’m just going to walk through some of the ways you can do that. How do you add
value? One of them is you could buy their product, it’s a great one. Buy their product.
Buy their high end product, whatever their top of the line product is, go ahead and buy
the product, use the product, tell them how you used it. People love to hear about
their customers, their clients getting results. Go tell them how you used to it, one of
the absolute best ways to get on someone’s radar. Another way is comment on their
blog. If they’re publishing a blog go and leave comments, leave intelligent comments.
You never want to be a stalker.

party, it could be a crowded party and 20 conversations going on in the room, but if
someone says your name across the room then somehow you can magically hear that
even among all these … Boom your name, boom, you hear it. It’s the same way when
someone sees someone wrote about them on the web or in social media.
There’s no way they cannot go there and read what you wrote. Another thing you

social media you could write about them and they will absolutely hear about you if you

Here’s you’re A players, your B players, your C players, your D players, your E players.

JV Launch, Part 3
So I walked through I identify potential joint venture partners and how to build value in
their lives, how to build relationships. Now I just want to talk about actually
contacting them. One of the things you really want to avoid is just sending a blind
email to someone or sending a blind social media, like a Facebook message or
something to someone if they don't know who you are.
First you want to build that relationship or at least put yourself on their radar, through
the ways I talked about in the last video but then the actual how do you approach
them. The best way to do this is at a live event. I love live events. I think they are
amazing for building your business for a lot of reasons, but one of them is the
networking with partners. There is no better way to meet someone than to like buy
them a beer or buy them lunch. That's the absolute best way to meet them and get to
know them.
If you can't get to a live event or they're not at a live event, the next best way is the
phone, and it can vary by the market and vary by the contact. A lot of people don't
love to get on the phone but the phone can be good, or direct mail like real physical
mail. But even better than direct mail is a FedEx. If you send a FedEx package to
someone, they're going to open that up.
Beyond that then you get down into the realm of social media or email. Absolute best
way to do this live event, meeting them face to face, go out to the bar with them, go
out to lunch with them, go out to dinner with them. Beyond that, you probably want to

do either phone or FedEx or just a regular old standard direct mail and the last place in

what a joint venture partner is. There are some markets, like if you know John
Gallagher, whose in that edible medicinal plants and herbs market that foraging, going

In that market he's had to educate people on what joint ventures are and how they

In my market, where I'm teaching people how to build their business online. Everyone

Then once you make contact and you're actually going, you've built value for them and
maybe you've promoted their product. When it's time to ask them to promote your
product, there are certain things that people are going to be looking for when they're
looking to promote your product.
First of all, they're going to want to know whether they can make some money,
whether you have a process that will actually convert to their leads. You can show
them your launch, that will put you head and shoulders above everyone else.
Show them the metrics on your launch. By metrics I mean how much money did
your internal launch make on a per-person basis on your list, based on the people on
your list, based on how many visits you sent to your pre-launch content, how many
unique visitors did you send your pre-launch content, what were your results per opt-in,
what were results. Show them the dollars per visitor, the dollars per opt-in.
The thing is when you go to them, you can say, "This is my internal launch, I can't
guarantee that your traffic will convert as well but this is a tested offer. It's a tested
sells process and these were my numbers. It's a tested launch. I did this launch. I did
an internal launch. It generated this many ... These kinds of dollars per subscriber."
When you go someone, you can share those kind of metrics then that will give them a
lot more confidence in your process. They're going to be looking for a process that
converts. They're going to be looking for that you've got a great product. You want to
show them your product, you want to show them testimonials, you want to show them
the results that your clients got.

Because if they're going to promote for you, their relationship is dependent on how you
treat the people they send to you. You want to show them that you're going to take
care of the people that come in and opt-in your list, you're going to take care of them,
you're going to give them good pre-launch content and then you're going make an offer
for a great product. If they buy the product, they're going to get great results.
They're going to care about the quality of the product, the quality of the conversion,
they're going to care that you actually pay them for their product. Now this might
sound amazing but it happens all the time, where there are people that ... You promote
for someone else and you never actually get paid. I've actually done this.
It doesn't happen all the time but it absolutely happens. They're going to want to know
and have the confidence that you are going to get paid. Basically this conversation is
all about what's in it for them. What is in it for them? Why they can trust you with
sending their traffic to you?
Why they can trust you with sending their list to your people? They want to know that
it's going to convert, that it's a great product, that you're not a slime ball, that you're
going to track properly and they're eventually going to get paid. All very, very
important considerations.
One other thing they're going to be interested in, they might be interested in, are you
going to be able to promote for them later on. May be promote for them first. There's
a lot of reciprocity, where one person promotes for the other person and then they turn
around and promote back. That happens all the time.
Those are some of the things that they are going to be looking for when they're trying
to decide whether they want to promote for you.
Another big one is, does the offer match their list? If they have a list about golf
and you come to them and you want to sell stuff about how to be a locksmith. It's not
going to be a good match, they're not going to want to mail, they want a good match,
they want their list to match up to your pre-launch content and to your product.
Keep all those things in mind when you go to talk to them, when you make that first
contact to see if they want to promote for you.

In any market, all these partners, they are all talking to each other. When they are

launch first.
